headline: Les Bleus Face Lions of Mesopotamia in Historic Tournament Debut
subheadline: France's quest for a third title meets Iraq's remarkable return after four decades away from football's grandest stage.
openingNarrative:
There are moments in football when the beautiful game transcends sport, when a single match carries the weight of nations, dreams deferred and destinies intertwined. When France and Iraq take the field in their opening group encounter, we witness one of those moments—a collision between expectation and aspiration that captures the very essence of tournament football.

For Les Bleus, this represents another chapter in their relentless pursuit of greatness. Having reached three of the last four tournament finals, winning twice and losing in the most dramatic circumstances to Argentina in 2022, France arrive as the world's third-ranked team with a squad value exceeding $1.6 billion. Kylian Mbappé, fresh from his Real Madrid coronation, leads a constellation of talent that stretches across every position. Yet beneath the gilded exterior lies a team desperate to exorcise the ghosts of Qatar, where they came so tantalizingly close to back-to-back glory.

Across from them stand the Lions of Mesopotamia, carrying the hopes of a nation that has waited forty years for this moment. Iraq's return to tournament football represents more than sporting achievement—it embodies resilience incarnate. Their last appearance came in 1986, but the memory of their miraculous 2007 Asian Cup triumph, won amid the chaos of war-torn Baghdad, reminds us that football's greatest stories often emerge from the most unlikely circumstances. With a squad valued at just $30 million, Iraq face the ultimate David versus Goliath scenario, yet their qualification through the grueling AFC pathway proves they belong on this stage.

tacticalBreakdown.title: The Chess Match
tacticalBreakdown.narrative:
Didier Deschamps will deploy France in their preferred 4-2-3-1, with Aurélien Tchouaméni and Eduardo Camavinga providing the midfield foundation that allows Mbappé, Ousmane Dembélé, and Michael Olise to wreak havoc in the final third. France's tactical versatility under Deschamps has always been their strength—they can dominate possession when needed or spring into devastating counter-attacks with Mbappé's pace as the catalyst. Their narrow pressing shape aims to win the ball quickly and transition at lightning speed, but it can leave spaces wide for opponents clever enough to exploit them.

Graham Arnold has instilled Australian pragmatism into Iraq's technically gifted core, likely setting them up in a 4-2-3-1 that mirrors France's structure but with vastly different intentions. Iraq will sit in a compact mid-block, using their double pivot to screen the defense while looking to feed Mohanad Ali through quick transitions. The Lions of Mesopotamia showed throughout qualification that they can frustrate superior opponents—their draws against Jordan and ability to grind out narrow victories suggest they won't simply roll over. Ali Adnan's attacking thrust from left-back and his set-piece delivery could provide Iraq's most potent weapon against France's high defensive line.
tacticalBreakdown.keyMatchup: Mbappé's pace versus Iraq's defensive discipline and organization

dataInsights.title: By The Numbers
dataInsights.eloAnalysis: The Elo gulf is stark—France's rating of 1870 places them among the world's elite, while Iraq's 1450 reflects their status as determined underdogs ranked 65th globally.
dataInsights.formAnalysis: France enter having won seven of their last eleven matches, including impressive victories over Brazil and Colombia in pre-tournament friendlies, while Iraq's qualifying campaign yielded six wins from ten matches with crucial victories over Kuwait and Oman securing their passage.
dataInsights.squadValueComparison: The financial disparity tells its own story—France's squad valuation of $1.636 billion dwarfs Iraq's $30 million, with Mbappé alone worth seven times Iraq's entire roster.
dataInsights.injuryImpact: France's only concern centers on Mbappé's left hamstring strain suffered in April, though he's expected fully fit; Iraq arrive with minimal injury worries, only monitoring Youssef Amyn's hamstring issue from their playoff victory.
dataInsights.bettingPerspective: Markets heavily favor Les Bleus, reflecting the vast gulf in individual quality and tournament pedigree between these sides.
dataInsights.historicalPattern: Iraq face the sobering reality that no team outside UEFA or CONMEBOL has ever won the tournament, though Morocco's 2022 semi-final run proves barriers can be broken.

playerSpotlights.name: Kylian Mbappé
playerSpotlights.narrative: The weight of French expectations rests squarely on those shoulders that have carried Les Bleus to two tournament finals. Now at Real Madrid and fully recovered from his spring hamstring concern, Mbappé arrives as both captain and talisman. His hat-trick in the 2022 final against Argentina announced him as football's present and future, but the pain of that penalty shootout defeat still burns. Against Iraq's compact defense, his pace and movement in behind will be crucial in unlocking what promises to be a stubborn rearguard action.
playerSpotlights.statline: Hat-trick scorer in the 2022 tournament final, France's leading scorer in qualifying with 4 goals

playerSpotlights.name: Mohanad Ali
playerSpotlights.narrative: The jewel of Iraqi football, Mohanad Ali carries his nation's dreams on his shoulders. At 25, he represents the generation that grew up inspired by the 2007 Asian Cup heroes, and his pace and directness could trouble France's high defensive line. Based in Qatar with Al-Duhail, he finished as Iraq's top scorer in their qualifying campaign with three crucial goals. Against France, he'll need to be clinical with limited chances, but his ability to drift wide and create overloads could cause problems for Les Bleus' narrow pressing structure.
playerSpotlights.statline: Iraq's leading goalscorer in qualifying with 3 goals, valued at $5 million as their most expensive player

playerSpotlights.name: Aurélien Tchouaméni
playerSpotlights.narrative: The Real Madrid midfielder has evolved into France's metronome, the player who dictates tempo and provides defensive screening in equal measure. His partnership with Eduardo Camavinga gives France the perfect balance of physicality and technical quality in midfield. Against Iraq's patient build-up play, Tchouaméni's ability to break up attacks and launch quick transitions will be vital. His Champions League experience and big-match temperament make him the player Iraq will struggle most to bypass in their search for creativity.
playerSpotlights.statline: Starting midfielder for Real Madrid and France, anchoring the double pivot that has reached three of four recent tournament finals

playerSpotlights.name: Ali Adnan
playerSpotlights.narrative: Perhaps Iraq's most accomplished player, Ali Adnan brings rare top-level experience having played in Serie A with Udinese and MLS with Vancouver Whitecaps. The attacking left-back's crossing ability and set-piece delivery could provide Iraq's best route to goal against France's defensive organization. His overlapping runs and technical quality make him one of the few Iraqi players capable of creating something from nothing. Against France's pace on the counter, his defensive discipline will be tested, but going forward, he represents Iraq's most potent creative weapon.
playerSpotlights.statline: Veteran of European and North American leagues, Iraq's primary set-piece specialist and attacking outlet

theVerdict.title: The Verdict
theVerdict.narrative:
The romance of Iraq's return cannot overcome the harsh mathematics of international football. France's individual quality, tournament experience, and tactical sophistication should prove too much for the Lions of Mesopotamia, regardless of their heart and determination. Mbappé's pace against Iraq's high line will create multiple opportunities, while France's midfield superiority through Tchouaméni and Camavinga will gradually wear down Iraq's resistance.

Yet football's beauty lies in its unpredictability. Iraq didn't wait forty years to return just to make up the numbers. If they can frustrate France early, keep the score respectable, and find Ali Adnan or Mohanad Ali in dangerous positions, they possess enough quality to create genuine moments of danger. But over ninety minutes, France's depth and class should tell. This won't be pretty, but it will be professional—exactly what champions do against determined underdogs.

alternativeScenarios.reasoning: Iraq's defensive discipline holds longer than expected, they score first on a counter-attack through Ali, and France need a late winner. Tournament football produces these kinds of scares for favorites.
Iraq executes a perfect defensive game plan, sitting deep and compact. France dominates possession but struggles to break them down until a single moment of Mbappé magic or a set-piece decides it.

closingLine: Sometimes in tournament football, the scoreline matters less than the story—and Iraq's return to the grandest stage, win or lose, is already a victory forty years in the making.
